<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Ichinomiya (Xinto)]][[de:Ichi-no-miya]][[en:Ichinomiya]][[fr:Ichi-no-miya]][[id:Ichinomiya]][[ja:一宮]][[ko:이치노미야]][[pt:Ichinomiya (templo)]][[simple:Ichinomiya]][[th:อิจิโนมิยะ]][[uk:Ітіномія (титул)]][[zh:一宮]]

[[File:Zenkoku-ichinomiya.jpg|right|thumb|List of ichinomiya all across Japan -- stone marker at Tamura Shrine in [[Sanuki Province]]]]
{{nihongo|'''''Ichinomiya'''''|一宮, also 一の宮 or 一之宮|first shrine}} is a [[Japan|Japanese]] term for the chief [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] in each of the pre-modern [[Provinces of Japan|provinces]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1120 "''Ichi no miya/Sōja,''"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200805230404/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1120|date=2020-08-05}} ''Encyclopedia of Shinto''; retrieved 2012-8-24.</ref>

The ''ichinomiya'' are the shrines with the highest shrine [[rank]] in an area.<ref>The provincial ''ichinomiya'' sometimes became place names, such as the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi]].</ref> Shrines of the lower rank are called ''ninomiya'' (二宮, second), ''sannomiya'' (三宮, third), ''shinomiya'' (四宮, fourth), and so forth.

== History ==
When [[Prefectures of Japan|prefectures]] were established in the [[Meiji period]], the old system of provincial ''ichinomiya'' was not changed.  Each new prefecture had one or more ichinomiya.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," pp. 1-3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref>
